claims.text: On a property in the Manastash Ridge near Ellensburg, Washington, a man calling himself Mel Waters claimed to have a nine-foot-wide hole in the ground that nobody — neither he nor previous owners — could ever fill, despite decades of dumping garbage, appliances and tires into it.

evidence.quote:
on melwater's property in the manastash
Ridge West of Ellensburg Washington
there's a hole in the ground

claims.rationale: The setup of the urban legend. There is no documented Mel Waters in Kittitas County records, and no such hole has ever been located despite extensive searching by multiple investigators since 1997. The 'unfillable trash hole' framing is the kernel of the legend — it has no independent physical evidence.

claims.text: Mel claimed to have measured the hole's depth at over 80,000 feet (more than 15 miles) by lowering successive 5,000-foot fishing-line spools without ever hitting bottom — a depth that would exceed any drilled hole in human history.

evidence.quote:
by the time Mel ran
out of line he was eighty thousand feet
down over 15 miles and still no bottom

claims.rationale: Physically implausible. A 15-mile-deep hole only 9 feet wide would have a length:diameter ratio orders of magnitude beyond what rock mechanics allows; the deepest borehole ever drilled (Kola Superdeep, 12.262 km / 7.6 mi) required massive engineering to keep the walls from collapsing. The Earth's crust transitions to ductile behavior well before 15 mi. The fishing-line measurement method is also unphysical: line drag, weight stretch, and friction would dominate long before 4 mi.

claims.text: The hole behaves anomalously: dogs and wildlife refuse to approach it, no insects or birds enter it, and a dead dog thrown into it reappeared days later in a wooded area — alive, still wearing its collar, but not recognizing its owner — suggestive of an interdimensional or otherwise paranormal portal.

evidence.quote:
no animals of
any kind near the hole no Wildlife no
insects birds even flew around it

a few days later that dog came back
after Mel's neighbor threw his dead dog
into the hole

claims.rationale: The 'resurrected dog' is the legend's first overtly supernatural beat. There is no corroborating witness, no name for the neighbor, no photographic evidence. The animal-avoidance framing has no measurement behind it. These details are exactly the kind of unfalsifiable add-ons that mark folk-horror radio narratives.

claims.text: Mel's hole entered popular consciousness because Mel Waters phoned in to Art Bell's late-night syndicated radio show Coast to Coast AM on February 21, 1997, where the program's reach (~hundreds of stations, ~10 million listeners) gave the legend its mass audience.

evidence.quote:
in 1997
there's only one place to discuss the
strange and mysterious and be taken
seriously Coast to Coast AM with Art
Bell

Coast to Coast was on hundreds of
stations around the world and had 10
million listeners

Mel
sent art a message on February 21st 1997

claims.rationale: Verifiable. Art Bell's first Mel Waters call did air on Coast to Coast AM on February 21, 1997 (and the show's ~500-affiliate / multi-million-listener reach is well-documented). The Mel Waters segments are catalogued in Coast to Coast AM's own archives.

claims.text: The day after Mel's first broadcast, US military personnel cordoned off his property, claimed a 'plane crash' had occurred (with no fire, smoke or wreckage), and a 'Man in Black' threatened to plant a drug lab if Mel didn't leave — implying the federal government was actively suppressing the story.

evidence.quote:
the day after the first broadcast
Mel was returning to his property but
the entrance was blocked

claims.rationale: There is no record of any military operation in Manastash Ridge in February 1997, no FAA crash report, and no journalistic confirmation. The 'Man in Black' motif is a stock paranormal trope going back to UFO researcher John Keel's 1960s books. As with c_0003, it is the kind of unfalsifiable embellishment that strengthens narrative drama but has no documentary support.

claims.text: A second hole, allegedly known to local Basque shepherds in Nevada for 200+ years, behaved even more anomalously than the Washington hole — radiating heat, lined with non-vibrating metal as deep as one could see, and capable of transforming ice into a substance that burns continuously for months.

evidence.quote:
a Native American tribe had contacted
Mel and asked him if he wanted to come
to Nevada

The Basque
settled a couple of areas in the United
States including Nevada in the mid-1800s

this hole was warm to the touch you
could feel heat all around it

it caught fire not only that
it continued to burn for months

claims.rationale: The Basque-shepherd-in-Nevada cultural backdrop is real (Basque immigration to Nevada/Idaho in the 19th century is well-documented and Basque sheepherding monuments dot the high desert). The hole and its 'ice that burns' behavior have no physical or chemical analog — phase-change of water ice into combustible solid is not a real phenomenon. The escalation from Mel's hole to the 'magic' Nevada hole is the legend's second-act expansion, designed to make the story more cinematic.

claims.text: Inside a sheep lowered 1,000 feet into the Nevada hole, Mel claims to have found a 'magic seal' fetus — a baby seal-like creature with human eyes — connected to a tumor by an umbilical cord; the encounter cured Mel's terminal esophageal cancer.

evidence.quote:
before Mel went to Nevada he was
diagnosed with Advanced esophageal
cancer he had only six months to live
but after this experience Mel was
cancer-free

claims.rationale: Self-evidently fictional. Spontaneous remission of advanced esophageal cancer happens but is vanishingly rare (~0.001-0.01%); attribution to a fetal-seal entity is not a medical mechanism. The biology described — humanoid eyes, umbilical-cord-to-tumor connection — does not correspond to any real organism. This is the legend's third-act peak, where the narrator (Mel) becomes a mythic chosen-survivor.

claims.text: After agreeing to return with photographic evidence and recordings of the magic-seal entity's spoken language, Mel Waters disconnected his phone and was never heard from again — a permanent silence that effectively ended the story without resolution.

evidence.quote: Mel is never heard from again

claims.rationale: Verifiable from Coast to Coast AM's own archive: Mel Waters made his final call in 2002 and did not return. Whether this reflects a hoaxer who 'painted himself into a corner' or something else is open, but the disappearance itself is a documented fact of broadcast history.

claims.text: No public record exists of any Mel or Melvin Waters living near Ellensburg, the hole has never been located despite years of search by hobbyists, and one promising candidate hole on Google Maps turned out to be a normal old well with a clear bottom — strongly indicating Mel Waters was a pseudonym and the hole does not exist.

evidence.quote:
there's no record of a male or
Melvin Waters living in the area

claims.rationale: Multiple independent searchers (including Coast to Coast staff, Skeptic magazine, Reddit hobbyists, regional journalists) have confirmed no Kittitas County records for a Melvin Waters of the right age and no found 'bottomless' hole. The Google-Earth candidate hole is a known location near the Manastash that turned out to be a shallow well. The pseudonym hypothesis is the parsimonious explanation.

claims.text: A 15-mile-deep, 9-foot-wide vertical shaft is geologically impossible: rock at that depth becomes ductile under lithostatic pressure and would close such a void on geologic timescales — so the literal 'bottomless hole' cannot exist by ordinary physics.

evidence.quote:
the hole itself just isn't geologically
possible a whole many miles deep would
be unstable and collapse in on itself

claims.rationale: Correct geophysics. Lithostatic pressure at 15 miles down (~700 MPa) exceeds the unconfined compressive strength of even the strongest crustal rocks; the brittle-ductile transition for granite occurs around 10-15 km. The Kola Superdeep required heavy drilling mud to maintain its 22 cm bore. An open 9-ft cavity at 15 mi would close rapidly. The host's verdict is essentially that of mainstream geology.

claims.text: Mel's Hole is best appreciated as a master class in how to present an urban legend on the air — Art Bell let the story breathe while still pressing on specifics, blending entertainment with mild epistemic challenge — and that grounded-but-curious approach is what most modern paranormal channels lack.

evidence.quote:
but for me in this channel
this was a master class on how to
present an urban legend

claims.rationale: Pure host editorial. The defensible part is the descriptive observation — Art Bell did push back on Mel (asking for photos repeatedly, surfacing skeptical callers' arguments) while still letting the story unfold; that interrogator-as-host posture is documented in his career. Whether 'most modern paranormal channels' fail at this is a judgment call but is consistent with the standards-of-evidence critique within the broader skeptical-podcast community.
